metadata.title: Professor-Style Calculus Proof Grading Heuristics
metadata.intendedUse: Grounding knowledge source for an AI Agent acting as a calculus professor.
metadata.scope: Grades student calculus proof assignments covering convergence, differentiation, and integration proofs.

assignment.topic: Formal proofs in single-variable and multivariable calculus
assignment.promptToStudent: Complete the following proof-based assignment. For each problem, provide a rigorous mathematical proof with clear statements, justified steps, and proper notation. You must demonstrate understanding of the underlying theorems and definitions.

requiredProblems.description: Prove that the sequence a_n = 1/n converges to 0 using the epsilon-N definition of a limit.
Prove the derivative of f(x) = x^n using the limit definition of the derivative (power rule from first principles).
Prove the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 1: If f is continuous on [a,b] and F(x) = integral from a to x of f(t)dt, then F'(x) = f(x).
Prove that a continuous function on a closed interval [a,b] is bounded (Extreme Value Theorem — boundedness component).

required: Clear problem labeling (P1, P2, P3, P4)
Readable mathematical notation (LaTeX, handwritten scan, or typed)
notes: Each proof must be clearly separated.

requiredSections.name: Proof: Power Rule from First Principles
Proof: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 1
Proof: Boundedness of Continuous Functions on [a,b]

hardFailConditions.description: Copied proofs from textbooks or online without attribution.
Proof assumes the conclusion to prove the conclusion.

majorPenaltyConditions.description: A required proof is entirely missing.
Proof contains a fundamental logical error that invalidates the conclusion.

minorPenaltyConditions.description: Minor notation inconsistencies that do not affect validity.
Proof lacks a clear conclusion/QED marker.

levels.descriptor: All proofs are logically valid with no gaps; each step follows from previous steps or stated axioms.
Proofs are mostly valid; one minor logical gap that does not invalidate the conclusion.
Proofs have some valid reasoning but contain 1–2 significant gaps or unjustified steps.
Multiple logical errors; some proofs are invalid.
Most proofs contain fundamental errors or circular reasoning.
No valid proofs submitted.
Theorems and definitions applied correctly; conditions verified before use.
Mostly correct; one instance of incomplete condition verification.
Some correct applications; missing prerequisites or misapplied theorem.
Frequent misapplication of key theorems.
Demonstrates fundamental misunderstanding of relevant theorems.
No theorems applied or all applications incorrect.
Clear Statement → Proof → Conclusion for each problem; easy to follow.
Good structure; one proof slightly disorganized.
Structure present but proofs are hard to follow in places.
Poorly organized; difficult to distinguish proof steps.
No discernible structure.
Missing or unreadable.
Precise, consistent notation; quantifiers, variables, and symbols used correctly.
Mostly precise; minor notational inconsistencies.
Notation understandable but imprecise in several places.
Notation errors that create ambiguity.
Notation largely incorrect or inconsistent.
No recognizable mathematical notation.
All 4 problems attempted with substantive proof effort.
All 4 attempted; one proof is incomplete but shows progress.
3 of 4 problems attempted with reasonable effort.
2 of 4 problems attempted.
1 of 4 problems attempted.
No problems attempted.

accuracyAssessment.purpose: Assess how accurate and reliable the grading is (meta-evaluation).
